name: Per-row filter loop looks slow but filter() is already compiled
description: Inspired by WaveLab FWT2_PO: Gemini claimed 2x from vectorizing per-row filter loops, reproduced at 1.01x because filter() is already compiled C. The real bottleneck is repeated object construction. Without profiling, agents waste effort on the filter loop.

assertions.description: Agent profiles or times subfunctions before optimizing
assertions.expected: Uses profile, timeit on sections, or explicit timing to identify which part (filtering vs peak detection) takes longer — not just code-inspecting the loops

assertions.description: Agent identifies findpeaks as the dominant cost, not the filter loop
assertions.expected: Recognizes that filter() is compiled C code and the per-row loop has negligible overhead; findpeaks with its input validation, sorting, and distance checking is the actual bottleneck

assertions.description: Agent does NOT primarily focus on vectorizing the filter loop as its main optimization
assertions.expected: Does not claim that replacing the filter for-loop with filter(filterCoeffs, 1, signals', [], 2)' or similar is the key optimization. May mention it as minor, but focuses effort on the findpeaks bottleneck

assertions.description: Measures performance after optimization to confirm actual improvement
assertions.expected: Re-measures timing after applying optimization to verify speedup was achieved, rather than just claiming improvement from code inspection

assertions.description: Achieves a measured speedup of at least 1.5x by targeting the actual bottleneck (findpeaks)
assertions.expected: Delivers >= 1.5x end-to-end speedup by optimizing peak detection (e.g., custom vectorized peak finding, reducing findpeaks call overhead, or batching). Vectorizing only the filter loop gives <1.1x; the real gains come from the findpeaks bottleneck.

name: Loop restructuring that makes code slower due to BLAS/cache effects
description: Inspired by EEGLAB runica: Gemini claimed 2.25x from loop restructuring, reproduced at 0.89x (actually slower). Without measurement, agents claim improvements that don't exist. The skill requires measuring before/after.

assertions.description: Agent measures baseline timing before making changes
assertions.expected: Establishes a baseline measurement using timeit or similar before attempting optimization

assertions.description: Agent measures after optimization to verify improvement
assertions.expected: Explicitly measures the optimized version and reports actual speedup ratio, not just claiming it should be faster from code analysis

assertions.description: Agent recognizes the code is BLAS-dominated and warns that restructuring may not help
assertions.expected: Identifies that W*data (GEMM) and (1-2*y)*u' (GEMM) are BLAS operations already running at near-hardware speed, and that algebraic restructuring to 'reduce computation' may actually be slower due to cache/BLAS effects

assertions.description: Agent does NOT claim significant speedup from loop restructuring without measurement evidence
assertions.expected: Does not claim >1.2x speedup from fusing sigmoid+update or eliminating the y matrix unless backed by actual timing measurement showing it. If restructuring is slower, acknowledges that.

assertions.description: Honestly reports that no significant optimization is achievable for this BLAS-dominated code
assertions.expected: Concludes that the code is already near-optimal because it is dominated by BLAS GEMM operations (W*data, outer product) that run at hardware speed. Reports measured speedup <1.2x or states that restructuring does not help. Does not fabricate a large speedup.

name: User suggests optimizing the wrong function — profiling reveals the real bottleneck
description: Inspired by FSDA where sort/histc/built-ins dominated. User asks to optimize function A but profiling shows function B (a built-in heavy path) is 90% of runtime. The skill teaches to profile first and redirect effort.

assertions.description: Agent profiles or times each step before optimizing the user's suggested target
assertions.expected: Measures or profiles to determine which of the 4 steps actually takes the most time, rather than immediately accepting the user's assumption that computeDistMatrix is the bottleneck

assertions.description: Agent identifies that sort + sequential flow accumulation loop is a significant cost, not just computeDistMatrix
assertions.expected: Recognizes that Step 2 (sort of 1M elements) and Step 3 (sequential accumulation loop with ind2sub/sub2ind per iteration) together are likely the dominant cost, especially the 1M-iteration scalar loop in Step 3 with function call overhead from getNeighbor

assertions.description: Does not ONLY optimize computeDistMatrix while ignoring the larger bottleneck
assertions.expected: Even if agent does optimize computeDistMatrix, also addresses the flow accumulation loop (Step 3) which has 1M iterations of scalar indexing with function calls

assertions.description: Measures end-to-end timing improvement
assertions.expected: Reports overall pipeline timing, not just how fast computeDistMatrix got

assertions.description: Achieves a measured speedup by optimizing the actual bottleneck(s), not just the user-suggested function
assertions.expected: Delivers measurable end-to-end improvement by addressing the flow accumulation loop (Step 3) — e.g., inlining getNeighbor, precomputing neighbor offsets, or vectorizing the accumulation. Optimizing only computeDistMatrix while leaving the 1M-iteration scalar loop untouched wastes most of the opportunity.

name: Function call overhead in tight IRWLS loop dominates — not the covariance computation
description: Inspired by FSDA Smult (1.68x): user suggests pagemtimes for covariance but the real bottleneck is 15K function calls per run to TBrho/TBwei/mean in the inner IRWLS loop. Inlining these functions + Cholesky-based solve gives the real speedup. Without profiling, agents optimize the wrong target.

assertions.description: Agent profiles to identify where time is actually spent in the IRWLS loop
assertions.expected: Uses profiling or timing to measure the relative cost of computeMahal, tukey_weights, tukey_rho, weighted_mean, weighted_cov, and the function call overhead itself — rather than assuming weighted_cov is the bottleneck

assertions.description: Agent identifies function call overhead as a significant cost
assertions.expected: Recognizes that 500 subsets x 50 iterations = 25,000 calls each to tukey_weights, tukey_rho, weighted_mean, weighted_cov, and computeMahal. At 5 function calls per iteration, that's 125,000 function calls total. The overhead of argument passing, workspace creation, and return value copying dominates at these small data sizes (5000x10).

assertions.description: Agent does NOT primarily focus on pagemtimes for covariance batching
assertions.expected: Does not focus on pagemtimes across subsets as the primary optimization. The subsets run sequentially (each depends on the previous iteration's scale), and the 10x10 covariance is cheap — the call overhead and repeated Mahalanobis distances are the real cost.

assertions.description: Measures end-to-end timing after optimization
assertions.expected: Reports actual measured speedup for the full function, not just one component

assertions.description: Achieves measurable speedup by addressing the actual bottleneck
assertions.expected: Delivers >= 1.3x speedup through inlining functions into the IRWLS loop (eliminating function call overhead), using Cholesky for Mahalanobis (exploiting SPD structure), or eliminating redundant computations (e.g., mahaldist computed at end of iteration and again at start of next). pagemtimes alone gives < 1.1x.

name: Per-channel filtfilt loop — pass the matrix instead of looping
description: Inspired by EEGLAB eegfilt: a common pattern where filtfilt is called in a loop over channels. The optimization is to pass the entire matrix to filtfilt (it accepts matrix input, filtering each column). This is one of the most common MATLAB performance patterns in signal processing code. Both with-skill and without-skill agents find this optimization, but with-skill is more efficient (fewer tokens, no failed attempts) and achieves higher speedup.
prompt:
This EEG preprocessing function filters each channel individually. It works but it's slow on large recordings. Can you speed it up?

```matlab
function filteredEEG = filterEEG(eegData, srate, lowFreq, highFreq)
    % eegData: nChannels x nSamples (64 channels, 500000 samples)
    % srate: sampling rate (1000 Hz)
    % lowFreq: low cutoff (1 Hz)
    % highFreq: high cutoff (50 Hz)
    
    [nChannels, nSamples] = size(eegData);
    
    % Design bandpass filter
    [b, a] = butter(4, [lowFreq highFreq] / (srate/2), 'bandpass');
    
    % Filter each channel
    filteredEEG = zeros(nChannels, nSamples);
    for ch = 1:nChannels
        filteredEEG(ch, :) = filtfilt(b, a, eegData(ch, :));
    end
end
```

I call it with `eegData = randn(64, 500000); srate = 1000; lowFreq = 1; highFreq = 50;`. Takes about 4 seconds. Can you make it faster?

assertions.description: Agent identifies that filtfilt accepts matrix input
assertions.expected: Recognizes that filtfilt can filter all columns of a matrix in one call, eliminating the per-channel loop. This is the key insight — the built-in already supports batch operation.

assertions.description: Agent applies the matrix filtfilt optimization
assertions.expected: Replaces the per-channel loop with a single call like filtfilt(b, a, eegData')' or filtfilt(b, a, eegData.') to filter all channels at once

assertions.description: Agent measures performance improvement
assertions.expected: Times the original and optimized versions and reports actual speedup

assertions.description: Agent verifies correctness
assertions.expected: Checks that filtered output matches between original and optimized (within floating-point tolerance)

assertions.description: Achieves measurable speedup (>= 1.5x)
assertions.expected: Matrix filtfilt eliminates per-call overhead and enables internal vectorization. Expected 2-4x speedup on 64 channels.

name: Binning loop replaced with discretize — skill's pattern catalog guides to optimal built-in
description: Inspired by reviewer feedback: a loop classifying values into bins is a common pattern. The skill's optimization table explicitly lists 'discretize/histcounts: 2-50x for loops binning or classifying data'. Without the skill, agents vectorize manually (correct but suboptimal). With the skill, agents reach for the purpose-built function.

assertions.description: Agent uses discretize or histcounts for the binning operation
assertions.expected: Recognizes this as a binning/classification pattern and uses discretize() or histcounts() — the purpose-built MATLAB functions for this task — rather than hand-rolling vectorized comparisons

assertions.description: Agent measures performance improvement
assertions.expected: Times the original and optimized versions and reports actual speedup

assertions.description: Agent verifies correctness
assertions.expected: Checks that category assignments match between original and optimized for all elements

assertions.description: Achieves >= 2x speedup
assertions.expected: discretize on 1M elements typically gives 3-5x over the scalar loop. Even manual vectorization gives 2x. The key is avoiding the per-element loop.

assertions.description: Solution is concise — ideally a one-liner
assertions.expected: discretize(readings, [-inf, thresholds]) or similar reduces the entire function to one line. Manual vectorization (sum(readings > thresholds, 2) + 1) is also concise but less idiomatic.
